title: Browser Automation for Billing Portals With Real Session State
description: Billing portals often depend on live account context and trusted browser state. Learn why local browser automation is stronger than detached sessions here.
keywords: local browser automation for finance tools
intro: Billing portals usually combine role-sensitive UI, tenant state, and live navigation context that detached browser sessions do not recreate cleanly.
problem: Teams automating billing portals often discover that the hard part is not clicking through forms. It is preserving the live session context that already exists in the operator's browser.
Fresh sessions introduce extra authentication, missing context, and duplicated setup work before the real task begins.
solution: A local browser runtime starts from the trusted browser session that already has the right account, permissions, and navigation state.
iatlas-browser follows this model by exposing the live browser through CLI, MCP, and a localhost daemon while leaving hosted APIs for public retrieval.
takeaways: Billing workflows are often session-sensitive.
Local runtime reduces setup friction in finance tools.
Browser state is part of the workflow definition.
Hosted APIs should stay outside these stateful tasks.
steps: Choose a billing workflow that already runs in a trusted browser.
Attach the local runtime to that active session.
Use snapshots and guided commands to stabilize the path.
Turn the stable sequence into a reusable local adapter.

title: MCP Browser Tools for Ops Investigation Workflows
description: Ops investigations improve when assistants can inspect the same browser session as the operator. Learn where MCP browser tools fit this work.
keywords: mcp browser tools for ops investigations
intro: Investigation work becomes more practical when the assistant can inspect the same browser surface the human is already using.
problem: Without access to the live browser session, assistants can only reason from partial evidence such as notes, screenshots, or logs. That weakens browser-heavy investigations immediately.
What teams need is a browser-aware MCP setup that lands inside the real session, not a detached sandbox.
solution: A local runtime exposed through MCP lets assistants inspect pages, capture snapshots, and navigate the same browser context the operator trusts.
iatlas-browser supports this with one shared local runtime across CLI, MCP, and the localhost daemon.
takeaways: Investigations benefit from shared browser context.
MCP is strongest when it reaches the real session.
A shared runtime lowers debugging and setup friction.
Hosted APIs remain for public retrieval only.
steps: Connect the assistant to the local runtime via MCP.
Use browser-aware tools in a real ops investigation.
Capture the useful steps for reuse.
Keep public research tasks on the hosted layer.

title: Local Browser API for Admin Workflows and Shell Automation
description: Admin workflows often need a browser API on localhost instead of a remote browser service. Learn why this pattern fits stateful browser tasks.
keywords: local browser api for admin workflows
intro: A localhost browser API is often the cleanest way to connect shell tools and browser steps without divorcing them from the session they depend on.
problem: Remote browser services cannot easily inherit the browser state an admin already has open, which creates friction in workflows tied to trust, permissions, and navigation history.
The task usually needs a stable local endpoint rather than another external dependency.
solution: A local browser API keeps control on the machine where the relevant session already exists, which is easier to integrate with scripts and local services.
iatlas-browser exposes this through its daemon while reserving the hosted surface for public retrieval and remote-safe adapters.
takeaways: Stateful browser control belongs close to the browser session.
Localhost is a strong control boundary for admin workflows.
Shell and local services integrate better with a local API.
Hosted APIs solve a different class of problem.
steps: Run the local daemon on the operator machine.
Test the health and command endpoints locally.
Keep stateful logic on that same machine.
Use hosted APIs only for public stateless tasks.

title: Hosted Page API for Public Research and Content Retrieval
description: Public research usually needs page metadata and text, not a local logged-in browser. Learn why this belongs on the hosted API layer.
keywords: hosted page api for public research
intro: Public research workflows usually need dependable retrieval from public pages, which is a hosted API problem rather than a session-aware browser problem.
problem: Teams gathering titles, metadata, and text from public websites do not need a browser attached to one operator's local session.
Using local browser automation for public retrieval adds complexity without improving the result.
solution: A hosted API keeps the retrieval surface narrow, remote-safe, and easy to integrate into research pipelines.
The `miaoda.vip/v1/open` endpoint fills that role while the local runtime remains focused on stateful browser workflows.
takeaways: Public research is a hosted retrieval use case.
Hosted APIs should stay narrow and predictable.
Local runtime should stay focused on session-aware workflows.
The product boundary is clearer when the layers stay separate.
steps: Register for a hosted API key.
Send public URLs to `/v1/open`.
Store metadata or text in the research workflow.
Use local runtime only when browser session state becomes necessary.

title: Site Adapters for Shared Browser Operations Across Teams

intro: Repeated browser tasks become easier to standardize when they are expressed as named adapters rather than scattered scripts or copied steps.

solution: Site adapters provide stable names, arguments, and execution rules for repeated browser tasks.
iatlas-browser already supports this through its site catalog and hosted subset while keeping session-aware tasks on the local runtime.

Execution mode should be explicit in every adapter.
Hosted adapters are for public remote-safe tasks.
Session-aware adapters should remain local.

Separate hosted-safe tasks from local-only tasks.
